original_abstract:
The $k$-Nearest Neighbors ($k$NN) algorithm, first introduced in 1951, serves as a cornerstone of modern data science, with applications spanning recommendation systems, the Internet of Things (IoT), and pattern recognition. As datasets grow in size and complexity, the traditional $k$NN approach faces significant hurdles, including high computational intensity and the "curse of dimensionality." 

This paper provides a systematic review and performance analysis of modifications designed to optimize exact $k$NN techniques, focusing specifically on $k$NN Search and $k$NN Join operations in high-dimensional spaces. We examine 31 $k$NN search methods and 12 $k$NN join methods, categorizing them based on their computing paradigms—I/O-based, main-memory-based, and parallel or distributed architectures. For each methodology, we evaluate its inherent strengths, technical limitations, and practical applicability. To support reproducibility and further experimentation, we provide the source code for the discussed methods. Furthermore, this study identifies persistent challenges in the field and outlines future research directions, such as the integration of ensemble learning and region-based searching to enhance classification accuracy and efficiency for large-scale data tasks. By synthesizing and comparing diverse methodologies, this survey offers a comprehensive guide for researchers and practitioners working with exact $k$NN techniques.
humanized_abstract:
Also known as K-NN (k-Nearest Neighbors), the k-Nearest Neighbors (kNN) algorithm was first proposed in the year 1951. It is one of the fundamentals of data science and is used in many of today’s products and services such as recommendation systems, and Internet of Things (IoT) applications, and pattern recognition. Although the simple version of the $k$NN algorithm is straightforward, it is severely limited when dealing with large data sets. For large data sets, the $k$NN algorithm can be quite expensive to run, and it is prone to the curse of dimensionality.

Approximate kNN search and join algorithms are proposed to enhance the exact kNN algorithms such as kNN Search and kNN Join. In this paper, a systematic review of the state of the art 31 kNN search methods and 12 kNN join methods with different computing paradigms like I/O-based, main-memory-based and parallel/distributed are introduced, along with their characteristics, corresponding applications and corresponding limitations. Corresponding code is provided to confirm the performance evaluation of each algorithm. Moreover, based on the above reviews, the bottleneck and future works of each method are introduced. In summary, in order to further improve the classification accuracy for large scale data and to accelerate the data processing speed, the combination of ensemble learning and region-based search is the research direction in the future work. In addition, this survey introduces various exact kNN methods in literature comprehensively and enables the researchers and the practitioners with a very clear and systematic understanding of the state of the art exact kNN methods.

document.output:

document.input:
The $k$-Nearest Neighbors ($k$NN) algorithm, first introduced in 1951, serves as a cornerstone of modern data science, with applications spanning recommendation systems, the Internet of Things (IoT), and pattern recognition. As datasets grow in size and complexity, the traditional $k$NN approach faces significant hurdles, including high computational intensity and the "curse of dimensionality." 

This paper provides a systematic review and performance analysis of modifications designed to optimize exact $k$NN techniques, focusing specifically on $k$NN Search and $k$NN Join operations in high-dimensional spaces. To support reproducibility and further experimentation, we provide the source code for the discussed methods. By synthesizing and comparing diverse methodologies, this survey offers a comprehensive guide for researchers and practitioners working with exact $k$NN techniques.
